data.conclusion: Validation passed: Finance grants unilateral authority for sub-$5k purchases; IT Security mandates assessment for ALL software with no cost threshold. The Finance authority is structurally incompatible with the IT Security gate.

data.topic: software procurement authorization and IT security review
data.conflict: SaaS procurement: Finance authority vs IT Security gate

data.conclusion: Affects all department managers with budget authority. Every sub-$5k SaaS purchase made under Finance authority is simultaneously non-compliant with IT Security. Shadow IT proliferation risk is active.

data.category: Governance Risk (authority gap between Finance and IT)
Security Risk (unevaluated SaaS tools in production)

citations.passage: Department managers are authorized to approve SaaS subscriptions and software purchases under $5,000 without additional sign-off.
citations.topic: software procurement authorization and IT security review

citations.passage: All software, regardless of cost or subscription type, must undergo a formal IT Security risk assessment prior to procurement. No exceptions.
citations.topic: software procurement authorization and IT security review

data.conclusion: Finance Policy explicitly grants autonomous sub-$5k procurement. IT Security explicitly eliminates that authority for software. Every such purchase is simultaneously Finance-compliant and IT Security non-compliant. Risk is MEDIUM — no imminent regulatory deadline, but shadow IT proliferation is an active risk.

risk_categories: Governance Risk (authority gap between Finance and IT)
Security Risk (unevaluated SaaS tools in production)
assessment.reasoning: Finance Expense Policy §3.1 grants department managers unilateral authority to approve software purchases under $5,000. IT Security Policy §11.4 mandates that all software — regardless of cost — must undergo IT Security risk assessment before procurement, with no exceptions. Every sub-$5k SaaS purchase made using the Finance-granted authority is simultaneously non-compliant with IT Security Policy. The policies cannot both be satisfied for the same purchase.

supporting_evidence.passage: Department managers are authorized to approve SaaS subscriptions and software purchases under $5,000 without additional sign-off.
supporting_evidence.topic: software procurement authorization and IT security review

supporting_evidence.passage: All software, regardless of cost or subscription type, must undergo a formal IT Security risk assessment prior to procurement. No exceptions.
supporting_evidence.topic: software procurement authorization and IT security review

affected_entities: All department managers with budget authority
potential_consequences: Shadow IT tools enter production without security evaluation.
Finance-approved purchases retroactively flagged as IT Security violations during audit.
Managers forced to choose between Finance policy compliance and IT Security compliance for each sub-$5k tool.
assessment.uncertainty: Volume of affected purchases is not quantified without procurement system data.

data.conclusion: Create a pre-approved SaaS vendor registry maintained by IT Security. Purchases from the registry satisfy both Finance authority and IT Security requirements simultaneously. Finance Policy §3.1 should reference the registry as a prerequisite. IT Security and Finance to co-own the registry within 90 days.

data.title: SaaS procurement: Finance authority vs IT Security gate
data.affected: All department managers with budget authority. Every sub-$5k SaaS purchase is simultaneously Finance-compliant and IT Security non-compliant.
data.deadline: 90 days — vendor registry must be established before next budget cycle
data.resolution: Establish pre-approved SaaS vendor registry maintained by IT Security. Registry purchases satisfy both policies. Finance Policy §3.1 must reference registry as prerequisite. IT Security and Finance co-own within 90 days.

data.conclusion: Finance Expense Policy §3.1 grants managers unilateral SaaS purchase authority below $5,000. IT Security Policy §11.4 requires IT Security assessment for all software with no cost threshold. Every sub-$5k purchase satisfies Finance policy and violates IT Security policy simultaneously.
